title.rendered: Roger Taylor: Racing in the Street

content.rendered:
“I loved that song, and I used to see Bruce play it live. But for me, it was always too slow, and I just wanted to do it mid-tempo, because it’s such a moving song. I felt that song, for me, should be faster. Bruce always did it so slow, and it was very effective in one way, but I just felt it would be nice if it moved along a bit.” — Roger Taylor, Goldmine, February 2015

I’m sure we’ve all thought that “Racing in the Street” would be more moving if it, well, moved more.

Right?

No?

Well, Roger Taylor did at least, and that’s what led him to record a cover of one of Bruce’s most achingly and beautifully poignant songs on his 1984 solo album, Strange Frontier–with a harder, faster spin on it.

Does it work? You decide.

© May 31, 2019

excerpt.rendered: Queen’s Roger Taylor picks up the pace in his cover of Bruce’s “Racing in the Street.” It moves faster, but is it still moving?

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roger Waters and Tom Morello: The Ghost of Tom Joad
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: One time only: Tom Morello teams up with Roger Waters and the Wounded Warriors Band for "The Ghost of Tom Joad."

jetpack-related-posts.title: July 6
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Bruce wraps up one Wembley stand, kicks off another, records performances for Live 1975-85, and more. Plus: Listen to a 1984 soundcheck that includes a never-played "The Good, The Bad, and The Ugly."

jetpack-related-posts.title: Amy Ray: Racing in the Street
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Long-time Springsteen fan Amy Ray loves "Racing in the Street" so much that she almost never covers it (out of reverence for the original). But she covered it last year, and you can watch her gorgeous performance inside.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me: Wild Thing
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: The E Street Band refuses to be stumped when a fan requests The Troggs' bar band classic.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Turn! Turn! Turn!
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Pete Seeger's soothing Bible verse adaptation became a classic in both its folk and rock incarnations, and Bruce paid tribute to both of them.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me, Roger Daltrey: Born to Run
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: It may have taken him two attempts to get started, but Roger Daltrey absolutely nails his "Born to Run" cover.
